question: Explain why 'Garbage In, Garbage Out' (GIGO) is a fundamental principle in data warehousing and describe one specific business consequence of poor data quality.
expectedPoints: GIGO means that flawed or low-quality input data will inevitably lead to unreliable, inaccurate output and analysis.
A data warehouse integrates data from multiple sources; if source data is poor, the entire integrated view is compromised.
A specific consequence is inaccurate financial reporting, leading to poor strategic decisions and potential regulatory fines.

question: Discuss three common data quality challenges encountered when integrating data from disparate source systems into a data warehouse.
expectedPoints: Inconsistent Data Formats: Source systems may represent the same data differently (e.g., date formats, measurement units), requiring complex transformation.
Lack of Standardization: The same entity (e.g., a customer) may have different identifiers or names across systems, causing duplication and inaccurate matching.
Missing or Incomplete Data: Critical data fields may be null or absent in some source systems, undermining the completeness and reliability of the warehouse.

question: Analyze how poor data quality can directly impact the various layers of a data warehouse architecture (Staging, Integration, Presentation) and the downstream business intelligence (BI) processes that depend on it.
expectedPoints: Staging Area: Poor quality source data (duplicates, errors) ingested here propagates through all subsequent layers, making cleansing resource-intensive.
Integration Layer (EDW): Low-quality data undermines the creation of a 'single version of the truth,' leading to inconsistent dimensions and untrustworthy facts.
Presentation Layer (Data Marts): BI tools and reports built on this layer will produce misleading analytics, eroding user confidence and leading to flawed data-driven decisions.
